headline: First-ever evidence-based baseline (Night 9): Dutch Senate passed two-tier asylum system April 21, 2026 — A-status (persecution) vs B-status (war/climate) with B-status holders denied family reunification rights for 2 years and restricted to legal spouses + minor biological children only; April 25 Loosdrecht violent anti-asylum protest prompted government to cut intake from 110 to 70 (yielding to violent pressure); April 10 ECOSOC vote elected Iran to UN human rights body — three independent in-window EQU/INT failures.
methodology_notes: First-ever evidence-based baseline (rotation-state showed null last_assessed). LIVE published composite 74.4 (rank 18, NOT 90.9 as flagged in rotation-state — see data-integrity flag for assessor reconciliation). The published scores are evidence-based Established substrate, but the cluster of three in-window April 2026 events warrants downward correction within the Established band. Primary anchor: April 21 Dutch Senate passage of the two-tier asylum system creating A-status (personal persecution) and B-status (war/climate) — B-status holders face minimum 2-year wait before applying for family reunification, restricted to legally married partners and biological/adopted children under 18 only, plus housing and income tests. Implementation set for June 12, 2026 to coincide with EU Pact on Migration and Asylum entry-into-force. Amnesty International, ECRE, RefugeeHelp, and Dutch Council for Refugees publicly called the law a violation of fundamental rights and a 'lottery of rights.' EQU drops most sharply (-12.5) — codified differential treatment of refugees based on flight reason is a structural EQU event. INT drops (-13.7) reflecting the gap between the Netherlands' stated humanitarian-leadership posture (and ECOSOC seat) and the legislative direction. ACC drops (-12.5) reflecting the April 25 Loosdrecht episode where authorities cut asylum intake from 110 to 70 in response to violent anti-asylum protest pressure — government accountability mechanism yielding to violent pressure rather than upholding humanitarian commitments. BND drops (-12.5) — narrower protection scope reduces the boundary of state responsibility toward war/climate refugees. SYS modest reduction (-6.2) — the Wilders coalition's broader migration restrictions package is a structural SYS signal. AWR/EMP/ACT modest reductions (-7.5 each) for the regression in stated humanitarian leadership. Calibration reference: the Norway pending proposal (-6.6, Apr 26 night) tackled an analogous Nordic refugee-rights regression; Netherlands has three compounding in-window events vs Norway's two, justifying a moderately larger delta. Composite 65.6 places Netherlands in the lower-middle of the Established band (61-80), consistent with its position as a Western European democracy with strong constitutional infrastructure undergoing active rights regression. Confidence high — the legislative event (Senate passage) is dispositive evidence of policy intent codified into law.

evidence.claim: Dutch Senate approved the two-tier asylum system April 21, 2026 — creating A-status (personal persecution) and B-status (war/climate) with B-status holders required to wait minimum 2 years before applying for family reunification, restricted to legal spouses and biological/adopted children under 18, plus housing and income tests. Implementation set for June 12, 2026.

InfoMigrants confirms the Netherlands has adopted a two-tier asylum system limiting family reunification — Amnesty International and ECRE urged delay; Dutch Council for Refugees called it a 'lottery of rights' and a violation of fundamental human rights.

April 25, 2026: Violent anti-asylum protests in Loosdrecht prompted authorities to cut asylum seeker capacity from 110 to 70 and delay opening until May 6 — government yielding to violent protest pressure to reduce humanitarian intake.

April 10, 2026: Netherlands voted at ECOSOC to elect Iran to a UN human rights policy body — directly contradicting stated human rights leadership posture; institutional vote tracked by Dutch and international press.

ECRE (European Council on Refugees and Exiles) and other rights bodies called for delaying the new asylum laws prior to Senate passage citing human-rights compliance concerns; Senate passed despite institutional opposition.

Visasupdate.com confirms full-implementation timeline (June 12, 2026) coinciding with EU Pact on Migration and Asylum entry-into-force — pattern positions Netherlands within broader European asylum rollback sprint (alongside Germany, Austria, Denmark, Norway).

key_evidence: Dutch Senate passed two-tier asylum system April 21, 2026 — B-status holders denied family reunification for 2 years; only legal spouses + minor biological children eligible
April 25 Loosdrecht: government cut asylum intake from 110 to 70 in response to violent anti-asylum protests — accountability mechanism yields to violent pressure
April 10 ECOSOC: Netherlands voted to elect Iran to UN human rights body — institutional contradiction of stated human rights leadership
Implementation timed June 12, 2026 to coincide with EU Pact on Migration and Asylum — part of broader European asylum rights regression sprint
Amnesty, ECRE, Dutch Council for Refugees publicly opposed law as violation of fundamental rights
Live composite 74.4 (rank 18, Established band) is correct evidence-based substrate; rotation-state drift to 90.9 is a data-integrity issue flagged for reconciliation
data_integrity_flag: Scanner queue and rotation-state.json reflected Netherlands at composite 90.9 rank 8 (Exemplary band). LIVE site/src/data/indexes/countries.json shows Netherlands at composite 74.4 rank 18 (Established band). Per assessor instruction, live composites are deterministic. The pending-proposals isolation list does not include Netherlands, so this proposal is permissible. Recommend rotation-state reconciliation pass to align with live data.
